coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of bifunctional macroporous adsorption resin via grafting carbon dot and application in the detection and adsorption of iron (III) ion
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hongwei
Huang, Chao
Ma, Shujuan
Guo, Shengwei
Gong, Bolin
Ou, Junjie
coredata.dc:description: The development of functional material is significant to remove heavy metal ions from water environment. Herein, macroporous adsorption resin (MAR) of poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene dimethacrylate) (poly(GMA-co-EDMA)) was selected as a substrate. Then, a functional monomer, 3-(triallylsilyl)propyl acrylate (TAPA), possessing three alkenyl groups, was grafted onto the surface of MAR via surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP). Subsequently, a kind of carbon dot (CD) with blue fluorescence fabricated with malonic acid and glutathione as precursors was anchored onto the surface of MAR to produce a bi-functional resin, assigned as MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD. Owning to fluorescence property of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD and presenting different fluorescence quenching for metal ions, especially for Fe3+ ions, the degree of quenching reached 46.2%. A linear range for 10–80 nmol L−1 (correlation coefficient of 0.9909) was acquired, meanwhile, the limit of detection about 9.74 nmol L−1 was measured, which could be applied in the trace detection. In addition, the functionalized MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was also utilized in adsorption of Fe3+ ion, and exhibited high adsorption capacity of 32.9 mg g−1. The equilibrium time for adsorption of Fe3+ occurred at 45 min. Thus, the bifunctional material can simultaneously achieve detection and adsorption of Fe3+ ion in aqueous solution, demonstrating great potential for commercialization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence detection
Adsorption
Bifunctional material
Macroporous adsorption resin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ion with MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD
Evaluation of the adsorption ability of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD for Fe3+ ions
Adsorption of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD for Fe3+ ion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support is gratefully acknowledged from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974137 ), the CAS-Weigao Research & Development Program ( [2017]-009 ), the Innovation Program of Science and Research from the Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics ( DICPI202005 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of Ningxia (No. 2021AAC02017 ) to J. Ou, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22164001 ) and the Key Research and Development Program of Ningxia (No. 2022BFE02002 ) to B. Gong, as well as Graduate Innovation Project of North Minzu University (No. YCX22157 ) to H. Wang.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic process of bi-functional material of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD and fluorescence quenching for Fe3+ ion.

SEM images of (a, b, c) pristine MAR and (d, e, f) MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD at different magnification. (g) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and (h) pore diameter distribution of MAR and MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD.
(a) FT-IR spectra of MAR, MAR@poly(TAPA), CDs and MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD. (b) XPS full spectrum of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD. High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) N 1s, (d) Si 2p and (e) S 2p.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum and (b) fluorescence intensity response of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD mixed with 13 kinds of metal ions. (c) Effect of concentration of Fe3+ ranged in 10–80 nmol L-1 on fluorescence intensity and (d) quantitative fitting curve of fluorescence quenching.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum and (b) relationship between Fe3+ concentration and fluorescence intensity of CDs suspension when diluted 2000 times.

(a) Adsorption isotherms of Fe3+ ion via applying MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD at room temperature. The fitting curve of static absorption via utilizing the (b) Langmuir and (c) Freundlich models. (d) Kinetic adsorption curve of Fe3+ ion with different adsorption time. (e) Pseudo-first-order and (f) pseudo-second-order kinetic plot for the adsorption of Fe3+ ion versus time.
Quantitative detection of Fe3+ ion via different reported CDs.
Comparison of adsorption property between several materials.

row.entry: Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer

head.ce:title: Fabrication of bifunctional macroporous adsorption resin via grafting carbon dot and application in the detection and adsorption of iron (III) ion

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory for Chemical Engineering and Technology, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, Ningxia Key Laboratory of Solar Chemical Conversion Technology, North Minzu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of functional material is significant to remove heavy metal ions from water environment. Herein, macroporous adsorption resin (MAR) of poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene dimethacrylate) (poly(GMA-co-EDMA)) was selected as a substrate. Then, a functional monomer, 3-(triallylsilyl)propyl acrylate (TAPA), possessing three alkenyl groups, was grafted onto the surface of MAR via surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP). Subsequently, a kind of carbon dot (CD) with blue fluorescence fabricated with malonic acid and glutathione as precursors was anchored onto the surface of MAR to produce a bi-functional resin, assigned as MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD. Owning to fluorescence property of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD and presenting different fluorescence quenching for metal ions, especially for Fe3+ ions, the degree of quenching reached 46.2%. A linear range for 10–80 nmol L−1 (correlation coefficient of 0.9909) was acquired, meanwhile, the limit of detection about 9.74 nmol L−1 was measured, which could be applied in the trace detection. In addition, the functionalized MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was also utilized in adsorption of Fe3+ ion, and exhibited high adsorption capacity of 32.9 mg g−1. The equilibrium time for adsorption of Fe3+ occurred at 45 min. Thus, the bifunctional material can simultaneously achieve detection and adsorption of Fe3+ ion in aqueous solution, demonstrating great potential for commercialization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The element of iron widely exists in the natural environment, which is essential for human body to carry out normal cell and organismal function [1]. However, unreasonable concentration of iron inside the human body is related to various diseases. Low iron levels in the body can lead to iron deficiency anemia, but the aberrant high iron levels lead to organ damage, arthritis, liver disease, and heart abnormalities [2–4]. Furthermore, a large number of accumulations of iron ions (Fe3+) in water can cause environmental problems [5,6]. Thus, it is of significance to develop effective strategies for detection and adsorption of Fe3+ ion in water environment. Conventional methods such as atomic absorption spectroscopy [7], molecular absorption spectrophotometry [8], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [9] and others are usually utilized to determine metal ions, but require expensive instruments, proficient operating skills, complex and time-consuming process of operation [10].

Carbon dot (CD) is a kind of promising fluorescent nanomaterial due to great biocompatibility, abundant surface functional groups and low toxicity [11–13]. CDs are defined by the characterized size of less than 10 nm and possess excellent photoluminescent properties [14]. Since the concept of CDs was presented in the years of 2004 [15], it was widely applied in the fields of biosensing [16], catalysis [17], biomedicine [18], bioimaging [19] and so on. It is remarkable that the CDs were utilized in the fields of detection of metal ions due to high sensitivity and selectivity. Recently, Song et al. [20] synthesized a multicolor N-doped CDs with red, yellow, and green emission via the participation of antibiotics aminosalicylic acid, which was utilized for detection of Fe3+ in living cells. Liu and co-workers [21] prepared B, N, S-co-doped CDs with red emission via one step hydrothermal treatment of 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid and 4-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride, and this nano-sensor was successfully utilized to detect Fe3+ ion. These typical examples illustrated the advantage of CDs for detection of Fe3+ ion, but they are not involved to the removal of Fe3+ ion.

Up to date, macroporous adsorption resins (MAR) have been widely utilized in the fields of separation and adsorption due to excellent mechanical strength, high porosity and stable chemical properties, accessible adsorption sites [22–24]. Especially for heavy metal ions, several resins were applied as carriers to remove heavy metal ions. For instance, Liu et al. [25] synthesized polyacrylic resin with a large number of functional groups via adding chitosan and dextran, which exhibited high adsorption capacity for Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+. Sun et al. [26] prepared a polystyrene resin coating with diethanolamine, further applying in adsorption of Cu2+ ion. In these typical examples, the grafting technique is adopted to achieve satisfactory functionality. CDs were popularly selected to graft onto several carriers. Thongsai et al. [27] utilized SiO2 as substrate to synthesize CD-containing hybrid material via thermal annealing method, which was applied in detection and adsorption of Cu2+. Xu et al. [28] fabricated a nanoparticle probe via covalently linking CDs onto the surface of silica nanoparticles, and further applied in determination of Hg2+ ion. Qiu et al. [29] selected citric acid and N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane to synthesize a silanizing CD, which was grafted onto spherical porous silica as stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography. Recently, polymers with vinyl pendants are also useful for functionalization via thiol-ene “click” chemistry, cross-linking, reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT), surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) and others. However, there were less reports on choosing asymmetric vinyl monomers with different reactivity to produce vinyl pendants [30].

Inspired by the above hints, herein, a kind of poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene dimethacrylate) (poly(GMA-co-EDMA)) MAR was chosen as carrier, and 3-(triallylsilyl)propyl acrylate (TAPA) possessing three vinyl groups and an acrylate group was grafted onto the surface of MAR via SI-ATRP. Owning to a large number of vinyl pendants, a blue fluorescence CDs could be quickly anchored onto the surface of MAR@poly(TAPA) via thiol-ene reaction at room temperature, and the resulting MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD could be applied in fluorescent detection and adsorption of Fe3+ ion.

Glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), ethylene dimethacrylate (EDMA), malonic acid, succinic acid, adipic acid, octanedioic acid, glutathione, and 2,2-bimethoxy-2-phenylacetophenone (DMPA) were purchased from Innochem (Beijing, China). TAPA, 2,2-azobisisobutyronitrile, polyvinyl alcohol, polyvinyl pyrrolidone, sodium dodecyl sulfate, triethylamine, 4-dimethylaminopyridine, disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate, 2,2′-bipyridine and 2-bromoisobutyrylbromide were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Standard aqueous solutions of Li+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, K+, Cu2+, Zr4+ and Fe3+ were prepared from LiCl, NaCl, ZnCl2, MgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, CaCl2, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, KCl, CuCl2·2H2O, ZrCl4 and FeCl3·6H2O, which were acquired from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Other chemicals were of analytical grade.

Initially, the poly(GMA-co-EDMA) MAR was synthesized with GMA as functional monomer and EDMA as crosslinker via typical seed swelling polymerization [31]. Then, the monodisperse MAR was modified via SI-ATRP and thiol-ene reaction, as presented in Fig. 1. Specifically, 1 g poly(GMA-co-EDMA) MAR was acidified by sulfuric acid (0.1 mol L−1) and washed with ethanol/water. After the obtained microsphere was dispersed in 32 mL of dichloromethane and stirred with 200 rpm for 30 min in an ice bath. Subsequently, triethylamine (1 mL), 2-bromoisobutyrylbromide (0.8 mL) and 4-dimethylaminopyridine (15.2 mg) were mixed in a round-bottom flask, maintaining the reaction system in the ice bath for 2 h. The acquired product was defined as macromolecular initiator and further utilized in the following step. Next, the macromolecular initiator (1 g) severed as carrier, and 2,2′-bipyridine (51.6 mg) was utilized as ligand and TAPA (0.5 mL) as functional monomer, which were together dispersed in the solution of ethanol/water (4/1, v/v). In order to avoid the effect of oxygen on the reaction, three repeated processes of freezing and thawing were utilized to exclude the interference of oxygen. Then, the catalyst of CuBr (26 mg) was added in the system. After further freezing and thawing process, the reaction system was set at 60 ℃ for 2 h. The product was successively washed with ethanol, deionized water, and an aqueous solution of disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (0.1 mol L−1). After drying, the product of MAR@poly(TAPA) with a large number of alkenyl groups was obtained.

The CD was synthesized via one step hydrothermal treatment [32,33]. First, 4 kinds of diacids (0.34 g malonic acid, 0.38 g succinic acid, 0.47 g adipic acid and 0.56 g octanedioic acid) respectively reacted with glutathione (0.5 g) according to a molar ratio of 2:1, which were completely dissolved in deionized water (25 mL). Then, these reagents were transferred to hydrothermal reaction kettle and reacted at 180 ℃ for 2 h. The acquired product was filtered via membrane and stored at 4 ℃.

CDs were anchored onto the MAR@poly(TAPA) via photo-initiated thiol-ene reaction. Briefly, MAR@poly(TAPA) (300 mg) and DMPA (30 mg) were dispersed in 4 mL of ethanol/water solution (1/1, v/v). Subsequently, 5 mL solution containing the as-synthesized CDs were added and ultrasonically mixed. The resulting suspension was transferred in a beaker, stirred with 200 rpm and irradiated at UV light of 365 nm for 2 h. Then, the product was washed by ethanol and deionized water, successively, and dried, which was assigned as MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ion with MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD
ce:section.ce:para: Thirteen kinds of metal ions (Li+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, K+, Cu2+, Zr4+, and Fe3+) were utilized to investigate the selectivity of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD suspension. The mixed suspension finally contained 100 µmol L−1 of metal ion and 0.02 mg mL−1 of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD. The mixture was shaken and reacted for 10 min after measuring the fluorescence spectrum at room temperature (λex=360 nm), and the fluorescence intensity of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was recorded at 437 nm. In addition, different concentrations of Fe3+ solution (10–80 nmol L−1) were utilized to quantitative analysis. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD), an important property of fluorescence materials for detection, was measured seven times with 0.06 mg mL−1 MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD suspension.

The fluorescence QY of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was measured by utilizing quinine sulfate (QY 55%) as a reference substance at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The quinine sulfate was dissolved in sulfuric acid solution (0.1 mol L−1) to serve as reference solution. The QY was calculated according to the following formula:
(1)
φ x=φ sⅹ(F x/F sⅹA s/A xⅹη x 2/η s 2)

where φ is the quantum yield, F, A, η represent the integral area of fluorescence intensity, absorbance and refractive index of solvent, respectively, and the subscript x and s represent the reference quinine sulfate and sample suspension solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the adsorption ability of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD for Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The standard curve of Fe3+ ion was measured via mixing 1 mL of diluted CDs suspension (2000 times) and a series of Fe3+ ion solutions with the concentrations of 5–300 µmol L−1, and the fluorescence spectrum was acquired with excitation wavelength of 360 nm at room temperature.

The adsorption capacity for Fe3+ ion was investigated via isothermal and kinetics experiments. For isothermal adsorption experiment, 5 mg of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD were mixed in 30 mL a series of Fe3+ ion solutions with 25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400 µmol L− 1 at room temperature for 5 h. The adsorbent MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was separated with centrifugation, and the supernatant was added in a diluted CD suspension to measure fluorescence intensity, so as to determine the surplus of Fe3+ ion in the supernatant. Compared with the standard curve line, the maximum adsorption capacity was calculated via following formula (2):
(2)
Q e = (C 0 - C e)V/m

where Q e is equilibrium adsorption capacity, C 0 and C e indicate initial and equilibrium concentrations of Fe3+ ion, respectively, V is the volume of Fe3+ ion aqueous solution, m is the mass of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD.
Furthermore, two typical fitting models of Langmuir and Freundlich were utilized to fit the obtained data of isothermal adsorption, which were shown as following:

Langmuir isotherm:
(3)
C e/Q e = 1/(Q m K L) + C e /Q m

Freundlich isotherm:
(4) logQ e =logK F + logC e /n

where C e (µmol L−1) is the equilibrium concentration of Fe3+ ion in supernatant, Q e (mg g−1) and Q m (mg g−1) are the equilibrium and maximum adsorption capacity of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD for Fe3+ ion, respectively, K L is the adsorption constant for Langmuir model, K F is the adsorption constant for Freundlich model.

For kinetics experiment, 5 mg of adsorbents MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD were added into 10 mL of Fe3+ ion (300 µmol L−1) solution. The fluorescence intensity of supernatant was determined at different time within 0–240 min, then the adsorption capacities at different time were calculated according to the Eq. (2), so as to the adsorption rate was revealed. In addition, the adsorption result was fitted by pseudo-first-order kinetic model and pseudo-second-order kinetic model as following:
(5) ln(Q e -Q t) = lnQ e - k 1 t
(6)
t/Q t = 1/k 2 Q e 2 + t/Q e

where K 1 and K 2 are respective rate constant of pseudo-first-order kinetic model and pseudo-second-order kinetic model, Q t (mg g−1) is the adsorption capacity in different time, and Q e (mg g−1) is the equilibrium concentration for Fe3+ ion.

The microscopic morphology and size were obtained via scanning electron microscope (SEM) of JSM-7500 F (JEOL, Japan). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) tests were utilizing a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI Tecnai TF20). The structural analysis was investigated by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) (X′Pert PRO MPD, Nalytical, Netherlands). Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) characterization was acquired by Thermo Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). The nitrogen adsorption/desorption measurement was performed on an ASAP 2460 physisorption analyzer (Micromeritics, USA), and specific surface area was calculated via the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were acquired by an ESCALAB 250XI XPS spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The fluorescence spectra were measured with a fluorescence spectrometer FluoroMax-4 (HORIBA Scientific, USA) at room temperature.

In recent years, MAR relies on the electrostatic interaction, hydrophobic interaction, size exclusion or hydrogen bonding interaction to achieve enrichment and purification of target compounds [34,35]. In addition, the adsorption capacity of MAR could be improved via introducing functional groups [24]. To our knowledge, there are scarce examples of grafting CDs onto the surface of MAR to serve as adsorbent. In our case, the synthetic process is exhibited in Fig. 1. The functional monomer TAPA was grafted onto the surface of MAR via ATRP to generate numerous poly(TAPA) pendants, providing many binding sites for following reaction. For the structure of TAPA, which contains an electron-poor acrylate group and three electron-rich vinyl groups, the different reactivity of vinyl groups and acrylate group determine the polymerization rate in the process of free-radical polymerization. It has been reported that the electron-deficient alkenyl group in the methacrylic acid functional group will be the first to polymerize, the conversion rate can reach 80% in a little time [36]. However, the least reactive vinyl groups can also involve in the polymerization reaction with relatively low polymerization rate [37]. Thus, moderate polymerization time (2 h) was suitable to generate the MAR@poly(TAPA) containing numerous vinyl pendants.

Subsequently, 4 diacids including malonic acid, succinic acid, adipic acid and octanedioic acid respectively reacted with glutathione to fabricate four kinds of blue fluorescence CDs. Under the same diluted times, the different fluorescence intensities and optimal excitation wavelength are presented in Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. S1a, the CD prepared by malonic acid and glutathione exhibited the strongest fluorescence intensity. Therefore, this CD was selected in following experiments and immobilized onto the surface of MAR@poly(TAPA) via thiol-ene reaction under irradiation of UV light (365 nm). The as-obtained material of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was applied in adsorption and fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ion. In addition, XRD was utilized to investigate the crystallinity and structure (Fig. S2). Obviously, a broad diffraction peak at 21.37o appeared, which was assigned to the (002) plane of graphite. This result illustrated the as-synthesized CD presented the amorphous and graphitic nature [38].

The SEM images of pristine MAR and modified MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD are shown in Fig. 2. The monodispersed MAR owned uniform morphology and size distribution, the average size was about 5.4 µm. The SEM images of pristine MAR are presented in Fig. 2a, b and c, the large pores on the surface of MAR are more beneficial to achieve modification and adsorption. Fig. 2d and e present the morphology of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD. Similar to the pristine MAR, the MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD surface became slightly rough. Furthermore, the morphology and size distribution of as-synthesized CDs were clearly observed via TEM. As presented in Fig. S3, the average diameter of as-synthesized CD was 2.04 ± 0.39 nm and almost exhibited spherical particle. In addition, nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiments were utilized to characterize materials, and the results are presented in Fig. 2g-h and Table S1. The specific surface area of MAR was 37.07 m2 g-1, while that of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was decreased to 23.34 m2 g-1 after modification, which was probably caused by grafting poly(TAPA) brush, and their average aperture was about 18 nm, which contributed to fast adsorption.

The stability of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD suspension is crucial for detection and adsorption. In order to explore the stability, 0.1 mg mL-1 of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD suspension was utilized to measure zeta potential, the absolute average values of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was 43.5 eV, which was higher than universally defined value (the absolute of zeta potential is greater than 20 mV [39]) of the suspension solution. It was indicated that the MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD suspension possessed great dispersion and stability, which was probably attributed to the negatively charged groups (such as -COOH), thus electrostatic repulsion from -COOH suppressed the aggregation of particles [40,41].

In order to clearly characterize the structure and functional groups of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD, the FT-IR spectra of MAR, MAR@poly(TAPA), CDs and poly(TAPA)-CD are presented in Fig. 3a. In the spectrum of MAR, the peak of 907 cm-1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of C-O-C from epoxy group, which came from the precursor GMA and disappeared in the spectrum of MAR@poly(TAPA), indicating that the epoxy group was almost exhausted out. A clear peak of 3532 cm-1 belonged to the stretching vibration of O-H, the peak at 1629 cm-1 was assigned to the bond of CC, illustrating the monomer TAPA was successfully grafted onto the surface of MAR. In the spectrum of CDs, the characteristic peak at 3450 and 3196 cm-1 belonged to the N-H and O-H stretching vibrations, while the peak at 2618 cm-1 was assigned to the S-H stretching vibration [42]. These results indicated that the CDs were fabricated successfully and possessed the groups of sulfhydryl and amino groups. However, these peaks disappeared in the spectrum of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD, which was probably related to less proportion of grafting CDs.

XPS was utilized to analyze the chemical construction of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD and MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD-Fe after adsorption of Fe3+ ion. The XPS full spectrum of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD is presented in Fig. 3b. The peaks of C 1 s, O 1 s and Si 2p could be clearly observed, and the peak of Si 2p at 102 eV was introduced from the functional monomer of TAPA. In addition, two weak peaks at 164 and 400 eV corresponded to the peaks of S 2p and N 1 s, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of N 1 s, Si 2p and S 2p of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD are presented in Fig. 3c, d and e. In the spectrum of N 1 s, the binding energy at 400.1 eV was assigned to C-N, and the N-H corresponded to 398.0 eV. The binding energy at 100.0 and 101.5 eV were corresponded to the Si 2p1/2 and 2p3/2, and the 163.6 and 166.8 eV were assigned to S 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 respectively. These results demonstrated the MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was successfully fabricated. Moreover, the XPS full spectrum of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD-Fe after adsorbing Fe3+ ion is presented in Fig. S4a, compared with the full spectrum of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD, the peak of Fe 2p was detected clearly. Fig. S4d shows the high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum, in which two binding energies at 711.5 and 725.9 eV corresponded to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, and the binding energy at 719.2 eV was assigned to satellite peak between Fe3+ ion and amine groups [43]. The high resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s are presented in Fig. S4b and c. The binding energies at 284.8, 286.2 and 288.6 eV were attributed to -C-C-, -C-O- and -CO-, respectively, and the binding energies at 531.8 and 533.3 eV were assigned to -C-O- and -CO- groups [44]. These results demonstrated Fe3+ ion could be absorbed onto the MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD. Moreover, the EDX images of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD are presented in Fig. S5 . The C, O and Si elements were clearly observed, but the spectra of N and S were slightly inconspicuous. Based on the XPS result, the contents of C, O, Si, N and S in MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD were 77.33%, 19.45%, 2.16%, 0.47% and 0.58%, respectively. Obviously, the C and O elements were mainly from MAR and CD, the Si element from poly(TAPA) layer, and the N and S elements from CD, illustrating successful introduction of poly(TAPA) brush into MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD.

Fluorescence QY of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was 5.9% by utilizing quinine sulfate as the reference, which is surpass to a N-doped CDs about 3.6% [45]. In addition, the anti-interference performance of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was investigated in the environment of different ionic strength. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity was not remarkably changed when adding NaCl aqueous solution (0.1–0.5 mol L-1), indicating the material of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD possessed great anti-interference performance [38,46]. The selectivity of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD toward Fe3+ ion was investigated. Thirteen kinds of metals ions including Cu2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zr4+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Na+, K+, Li+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+ were mixed with MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD suspension. After measuring with fluorescence spectroscopy at the response wavelength of 437 nm, the relationship between different metal ions with fluorescence intensity is presented in Fig. 4a and b. The fluorescence intensity could change with the addition of different metal ions. Firstly, the intensity were increased slightly by adding Mg2+ and Zn2+ ions. Compared with the intensity of blank experiment, the fluorescence intensities almost remained when adding Pb2+. In addition, the quenching degree of 6 kinds of metal ions including Ca2+, Cr3+, Li+, Na+, Zr4+ and K+ were declined slightly. Notably, 4 kinds of metal ions including Cd2+, Co3+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ exhibited higher quenching degree and reached 31.45%, 32.11%, 38.61% and 46.16%, respectively. Obviously, among these metal ions, the maximum efficiency of fluorescence quenching was obtained when adding Fe3+ ion. Thus, the MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was further utilized to quantitative analysis of Fe3+ ion.

The sensing ability of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD for Fe3+ ion was investigated in the range of 10–80 nmol L-1. As shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity at 437 nm gradually decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. A satisfactory correlation coefficient (R=0.9909) was acquired (Fig. 4d). Meanwhile, the LOD of 9.74 nmol L-1 acquired. Recently, various CDs were developed to detect Fe3+ ion. Table 1 lists the detection ability of several CDs. Compared with our work, these CDs exhibited unsatisfactory LOD, especially blueberry CDs [47]. The excellent sensitivity of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD is beneficial to achieve trace detection of Fe3+ ion, which could be related on the stability of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD, avoiding the agglomeration of CDs.

The process of fluorescence quenching was related on amino groups [51], and further collaborate with the oxygen-containing groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. These functional groups combined with Fe3+ ion to form the complex MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD-Fe via the interactions of chelation and coordination, and the fluorescence quenching occurred by electron-transfer [52,53]. This mechanism of fluorescence quenching mainly depends on the interaction of chemical bonding with MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD-Fe, the electrons of excited state are transferred to the 3d orbital of Fe3+ ion after Fe3+ ion is chelated onto the surface of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD-Fe [54]. In addition, the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups have the excellent affinity capacity of binding for Fe3+ ion, which is stronger than other metal ions [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD for Fe3+ ion
ce:section.ce:para: The removal of Fe3+ ion from water environment is essential process due to its effects on human health. At present, several kinds of materials such as ion-imprinted material [56], chitosan [6,57], metal-organic frameworks [58], biopolymer [59] were utilized to separate Fe3+ ion in body fluids or aqueous environment. In this work, the nanoscale CDs were grafted onto the surface of MAR to fabricate the bifunctional material of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD, which was also utilized as adsorbent to separate Fe3+ ion from aqueous solution. Initially, the fluorescence intensity was measured at λex= 360 nm, and the relationship between fluorescence intensity of CDs and the concentration of Fe3+ ion (5–300 µmol L-1) was also investigated ( Fig. 5a). Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 5b, two linear fitting curves in the range of 5–25 µmol L-1 and 25–300 µmol L-1 were obtained, and the linear correlation coefficients were 0.995 and 0.992, respectively. The acquired two fitting curves could be served as standard curves to calculate the adsorption amount of Fe3+ ion. As presented in Fig. 6a, the adsorption amount was quickly increased in a range of 25–100 µmol L-1, and the maximum adsorption capacity reached 32.9 mg g-1 when the Fe3+ ion concentration at 200 µmol L-1. The acquired data were fitted by Langmuir and Freundlich models, as shown in Fig. 6b and c, the linear correlation coefficients were 0.9909 and 0.7748, respectively. The results indicated the adsorption process was more suitable to Langmuir model and the process is monolayer adsorption.

Kinetic adsorption curve is exhibited in Fig. 6d, the adsorption capacity was rapidly increased within 30 min. The equilibrium was achieved at 45 min and the absorption capacity reached 27.95 mg g-1, which demonstrated the material of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD owned faster adsorption rate. Meanwhile, the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics models were utilized to evaluate the dynamic adsorption process. As shown in Fig. 6e and f, the coefficient of pseudo-first-order was 0.7441 (R1), while pseudo-second-order was 0.9962 (R2). Obviously, the dynamic adsorption process was more suitable for pseudo-second-order model. These results were probably related to the metal chelating effect between amino/carboxyl group and Fe3+ ion, indicating the adsorption process is chemical adsorption.

Various materials were recently developed to remove Fe3+ ion, as shown in Table 2. The adsorption capacity of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was superior to the ion-imprinted polymer [62,63] and cow bone charcoal [61], which could be related on the chelation ability between Fe3+ ion and groups (amino and carboxyl) from the CD. In addition, the adsorption rate of MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD for Fe3+ ion was rapid and the equilibrium reached at 45 min, which was faster than chitosan beads [60] and ion-imprinted polymer [62]. In our recent strategy [64], the fluorescence spectrometer was proven to be a fast and simple method to measure the concentration of Fe3+ ion. Thus, the MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD exhibited dual-functionality of adsorption and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ion.

As a significant strategy of modification, plentiful poly(TAPA) pendants containing vinyl groups have been successfully grafted onto the surface of MAR via ATRP, providing more possibility for subsequent modifications. Thus, a CD with thiol functional group was facilely anchored onto the surface of MAR@poly(TAPA) via thiol-ene “clickˮ reaction. The as-fabricated MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD possessed excellent dispersion and solubility in aqueous solution could be utilized as bi-functional material for detection and adsorption for Fe3+ ion. Rapidly adsorption equilibrium was finished within 45 min, and the maximum adsorption capacity reached 32.9 mg g-1, illustrating the bifunctional material MAR@poly(TAPA)-CD was a promising tool in the fields of adsorption and detection for Fe3+ ion in aqueous environment. Thus, the strategy of CD-modification may offer more excellent properties and functions, which can be transferred in various carriers and applied in more fields, such as biomedicine, electronics, sensing and other fields. Moreover, due to easy large-scale production of MAR, it is anticipated that this kind of material will be commercialized and widely utilized in more fields.

Hongwei Wang: Experimental operation, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Chao Huang: Formal analysis. Shujuan Ma: Project administration. Shengwei Guo: Supervision. Bolin Gong: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Junjie Ou: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Ou, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22164001) and the Key Research and Development Program of Ningxia (No. 2022BFE02002) to B. ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2022.105220.

Lee, Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink, Biosens. Bioelectron. 99 (2018) 303–311.
